\begin{document}

\begin{center}
    {\LARGE \bfseries Series Solutions of Differential Equations}\\[6pt]
    {\large Prepared by Staples Education}\\[4pt]
    {\itshape Condensed Cheat Sheet: Power Series, Frobenius, and the Classical Special Functions}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{topicsbox}[Unit 13 at a Glance: Series Solutions]
Most differential equations have no closed-form elementary solution. The series method assumes the solution is an (possibly singular) analytic function $y = \sum a_n x^n$, substitutes it into the equation, and matches coefficients of each power of $x$ to extract a recurrence for the $a_n$. At an ordinary point a plain power series works; at a regular singular point the Frobenius factor $x^r$ is required. Applied to the great equations of mathematical physics, the method manufactures the classical special functions.

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \textbf{Taylor foundations} --- the coefficient formula $f^{(n)}(a)/n!$, the standard library ($e^x,\sin,\cos,\tfrac{1}{1-x}$), and the radius of convergence.
    \item \textbf{Power series solutions} --- ordinary points, the ansatz $y=\sum a_n x^n$, re-indexing the derivatives, and reading off a recurrence.
    \item \textbf{The Frobenius method} --- regular singular points, the ansatz $x^r\sum a_n x^n$, the indicial equation, and its three root cases.
    \item \textbf{Legendre and Bessel} --- the two flagship equations, polynomial versus transcendental solutions, and orthogonality.
    \item \textbf{Hermite, Laguerre, Chebyshev} --- three more orthogonal families, their weights, recurrences, and physical homes.
\end{itemize}
\end{topicsbox}

\vspace{0.6em}
\hrule
\vspace{0.6em}

\phantomsection

\section*{Part I --- Condensed Cheat Sheet}
\setcounter{section}{13}   % subsections auto-number as the Unit 13 modules

% ---- Module 13.1 ----------------------------------------
\subsection{Taylor Series and the Exponential Function}
\emph{Taylor and Maclaurin expansions, the coefficient formula $f^{(n)}(a)/n!$, the standard library of series, and the radius of convergence via the ratio test.}

\begin{formulabox}[Taylor / Maclaurin Series]
\[ f(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{f^{(n)}(a)}{n!}(x-a)^n, \qquad \text{Maclaurin: } a = 0. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Standard Library]
\[ e^x = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{x^n}{n!}, \quad \cos x = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n x^{2n}}{(2n)!}, \quad \sin x = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n x^{2n+1}}{(2n+1)!}, \quad \frac{1}{1-x} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^n \ (|x|<1). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Radius of Convergence (Ratio Test)]
\[ \frac{1}{R} = \lim_{n\to\infty}\left|\frac{a_{n+1}}{a_n}\right|, \qquad \text{series converges for } |x-a| < R. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Build a Maclaurin Series]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Compute the derivatives $f^{(n)}(0)$ at the center.
    \item Form the coefficients $a_n = f^{(n)}(0)/n!$.
    \item Assemble the series $\sum_{n\ge 0} a_n x^n$.
    \item Determine the radius of convergence with the ratio test.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
An \textcolor{accentorange}{analytic function equals its own Taylor series} inside the radius of convergence $R$. This is the licence for the entire unit: if a solution is analytic, positing $y=\sum a_n x^n$ loses nothing.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
A Taylor series only represents $f$ \emph{inside} its radius of convergence. The geometric series $\sum x^n$ equals $\tfrac{1}{1-x}$ only for $|x|<1$; plugging in $x=2$ is meaningless even though $\tfrac{1}{1-x}$ is perfectly finite there.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

% ---- Module 13.2 ----------------------------------------
\subsection{Power Series Solutions at an Ordinary Point}
\emph{Ordinary points, the ansatz $y=\sum a_n x^n$, term-by-term differentiation and re-indexing, and extracting a recurrence relation for the coefficients.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Ansatz and Its Derivatives (re-indexed to $x^n$)]
\[ y = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n, \quad y' = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(n+1)a_{n+1}x^n, \quad y'' = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(n+2)(n+1)a_{n+2}x^n. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Ordinary Point Test]
\[ \text{For } y'' + p(x)y' + q(x)y = 0,\ \ x_0 \text{ is ordinary} \iff p,q \text{ are both analytic at } x_0. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Guaranteed Radius]
\[ R \ge \text{distance from } x_0 \text{ to the nearest singular point in the complex plane.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Series Solution at an Ordinary Point]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Confirm $x_0$ is an ordinary point ($p,q$ analytic there).
    \item Substitute $y=\sum a_n x^n$ and the re-indexed derivatives into the equation.
    \item Shift indices so every term carries the common power $x^n$.
    \item Match coefficients of $x^n$ to obtain a recurrence relation.
    \item Solve the recurrence; $a_0$ and $a_1$ remain free and generate the two independent solutions.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
At an ordinary point the two free constants \textcolor{accentorange}{$a_0$ and $a_1$} are precisely the two arbitrary constants of a second-order ODE — the even-indexed and odd-indexed coefficients build the two independent solutions.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
You must \emph{re-index} every series to the same power $x^n$ before matching coefficients. Skipping the index shift after differentiating (forgetting the $(n+1)$ or $(n+2)(n+1)$ factor) is the single most common source of a wrong recurrence.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

% ---- Module 13.3 ----------------------------------------
\subsection{The Frobenius Method}
\emph{Regular singular points, the Frobenius ansatz $x^r\sum a_n x^n$, the indicial equation $r(r-1)+p_0 r+q_0=0$, and the three cases of its roots.}

\begin{formulabox}[Regular Singular Point]
\[ x_0 \text{ is singular, yet } (x-x_0)\,p(x) \text{ and } (x-x_0)^2 q(x) \text{ are analytic at } x_0. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Frobenius Ansatz and Indicial Equation]
\[ y = x^r\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a_n x^n, \qquad r(r-1) + p_0\,r + q_0 = 0, \quad p_0=\lim_{x\to 0} x\,p(x),\ q_0=\lim_{x\to 0} x^2 q(x). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Three Root Cases ($r_1 \ge r_2$)]
\[ \begin{aligned} &\text{(i) } r_1-r_2 \notin \mathbb{Z}: && \text{two clean Frobenius series.} \\ &\text{(ii) } r_1=r_2: && \text{second solution needs a } \ln x \text{ term.} \\ &\text{(iii) } r_1-r_2\in\mathbb{Z}^+: && \ln x \text{ may appear.} \end{aligned} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- The Frobenius Procedure]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Confirm $x_0$ is a regular singular point ($xp$ and $x^2q$ analytic).
    \item Compute $p_0=\lim x\,p(x)$ and $q_0=\lim x^2 q(x)$.
    \item Form and solve the indicial equation $r(r-1)+p_0 r+q_0=0$ for $r_1\ge r_2$.
    \item Substitute $y=\sum a_n x^{n+r}$ with the larger root to get a recurrence for $a_n$.
    \item Build the second solution according to the root case (a log term if roots coincide or differ by an integer).
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The Frobenius exponent \textcolor{accentorange}{$x^r$} is exactly what captures the singular behavior a plain power series cannot. The indicial equation comes from the \emph{lowest} power of $x$ in the substitution, and its roots set the leading exponents of the two solutions.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
When the indicial roots are equal, or differ by a positive integer, the two series can collide. Do not assume two clean series — the smaller-root solution may require a \textcolor{warnred}{logarithmic term} $y_1\ln x$ for independence.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

% ---- Module 13.4 ----------------------------------------
\subsection{Legendre and Bessel Equations}
\emph{The two flagship equations of mathematical physics, the contrast between terminating polynomial solutions and transcendental ones, and orthogonality.}

\begin{formulabox}[Legendre's Equation]
\[ (1-x^2)y'' - 2x y' + n(n+1)y = 0, \quad \text{singular at } x=\pm 1,\ \text{ordinary at } x=0. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Legendre Polynomials and Orthogonality]
\[ P_0=1,\ P_1=x,\ P_2=\tfrac{1}{2}(3x^2-1); \qquad \int_{-1}^{1} P_m(x)P_n(x)\,dx = \frac{2}{2n+1}\,\delta_{mn}. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Bessel's Equation of Order $\nu$]
\[ x^2 y'' + x y' + (x^2 - \nu^2)y = 0, \quad \text{regular singular at } x=0,\ \text{indicial roots } r = \pm\nu. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Recognize and Solve a Named Equation]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Match the equation to its standard form and read off the parameter ($n$ or $\nu$).
    \item Classify $x=0$: Legendre is ordinary there (use a power series); Bessel is regular singular (use Frobenius).
    \item For Legendre with integer $n$, the series terminates into the degree-$n$ polynomial $P_n$.
    \item For Bessel, the larger root $+\nu$ gives the bounded first-kind solution $J_\nu$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The \textcolor{accentorange}{special functions} are simply the named solutions of the physically important singular ODEs. Legendre arises from spherical symmetry and terminates into polynomials for integer $n$; Bessel arises from cylindrical symmetry and gives the oscillatory $J_\nu$.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Bessel's equation at $x=0$ is a \emph{regular singular point}, not an ordinary point — the coefficients blow up there. A plain power series fails; you must use Frobenius with indicial roots $r=\pm\nu$.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

% ---- Module 13.5 ----------------------------------------
\subsection{Hermite, Laguerre, and Chebyshev Polynomials}
\emph{Three more classical orthogonal families: their differential equations, recurrences, weight functions, and the physical or numerical settings where each appears.}

\begin{formulabox}[Hermite (physicists' convention)]
\[ y'' - 2x y' + 2n y = 0; \quad H_0=1,\ H_1=2x,\ H_2=4x^2-2; \quad \text{weight } e^{-x^2} \text{ on } (-\infty,\infty). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Laguerre]
\[ x y'' + (1-x)y' + n y = 0; \quad \text{weight } e^{-x} \text{ on } [0,\infty). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Chebyshev (first kind)]
\[ \begin{gathered} (1-x^2)y'' - x y' + n^2 y = 0; \quad T_n(\cos\theta)=\cos(n\theta) \\ T_0=1,\ T_1=x,\ T_2=2x^2-1; \quad \text{weight } \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}. \end{gathered} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Place a Classical Family]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Inspect the leading coefficient and the first-derivative term to fingerprint the equation.
    \item Match to the family: constant lead with $-2xy'$ is Hermite; $xy''$ with $(1-x)y'$ is Laguerre; $(1-x^2)y''$ with a single $xy'$ is Chebyshev.
    \item Generate the polynomials from the family's recurrence.
    \item Pair with the correct weight function for orthogonality.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Each classical family is an \textcolor{accentorange}{orthogonal basis under its own weight}, tailored to a geometry: Hermite ($e^{-x^2}$) to the quantum harmonic oscillator, Laguerre ($e^{-x}$) to the hydrogen atom, Chebyshev ($1/\sqrt{1-x^2}$) to minimax approximation.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Do not confuse the degree-2 members: \textcolor{warnred}{$H_2 = 4x^2-2$} (Hermite), $T_2 = 2x^2-1$ (Chebyshev), and $P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}(3x^2-1)$ (Legendre) all look similar but come from different equations, recurrences, and weights.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

\begin{conceptbox}[Unit Key Takeaway]
The series method is one idea applied with increasing nerve: \textcolor{accentorange}{posit an analytic solution} $y=\sum a_n x^n$, substitute, and match powers to get a recurrence. At an ordinary point a plain power series works; at a regular singular point the \textcolor{accentorange}{Frobenius factor $x^r$} and its indicial equation are required. Run the machine on the great equations of physics and out come the Legendre, Bessel, Hermite, Laguerre, and Chebyshev families --- each an orthogonal basis under its own weight.
\end{conceptbox}

\end{document}
